cards.front: What is the difference between a stack and a queue?
cards.back: Stack: Last-In-First-Out (LIFO). Operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top). Queue: First-In-First-Out (FIFO). Operations: enqueue (add to back), dequeue (remove from front).
cards.notes: Stack use cases: function call stack, undo operations, parsing. Queue use cases: BFS, task scheduling, print queue.

cards.back: Describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time or space complexity as input grows. Focuses on the worst case and dominant terms. Common: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic, O(2ⁿ) exponential.
cards.notes: Drop constants and lower-order terms. O(3n + 5) → O(n). O(n² + n) → O(n²).

cards.front: What is the difference between an array and a linked list?
cards.back: Array: Contiguous memory, O(1) random access, O(n) insertion/deletion (shifting). Linked List: Non-contiguous memory (nodes with pointers), O(n) access, O(1) insertion/deletion (if at known position).
cards.notes: Array: better for cache performance and random access. Linked list: better for frequent insertions/deletions at arbitrary positions.

cards.front: What is a hash table and how does it work?
cards.back: Data structure mapping keys to values using a hash function. Hash function converts key → index in an array. Average case: O(1) for insert, delete, lookup. Worst case: O(n) with many collisions.
cards.notes: Collision resolution: chaining (linked lists at each bucket) or open addressing (probing). Load factor = n/k. Resize when load factor exceeds threshold.

cards.front: What is recursion? What are its key components?
cards.back: A function that calls itself to solve smaller subproblems. Key components: 1) Base case (termination condition), 2) Recursive case (problem reduction toward base case). Each call adds a frame to the call stack.
cards.notes: Risk: stack overflow if base case is missing or unreachable. Tail recursion: recursive call is the last operation (can be optimized by compilers).

cards.front: What is the difference between HTTP GET and POST?
cards.back: GET: Retrieve data. Parameters in URL. Idempotent, cacheable, bookmarkable. Body not typically used. POST: Submit data. Parameters in request body. Not idempotent, not cached. Used for creating resources, form submissions.
cards.notes: REST: GET = read, POST = create, PUT = update/replace, PATCH = partial update, DELETE = remove.

cards.front: What is a binary search tree (BST)?
cards.back: A binary tree where for each node: all left subtree values < node value, all right subtree values > node value. Operations (balanced): O(log n) for search, insert, delete.
cards.notes: Unbalanced BST can degrade to O(n) — essentially a linked list. Self-balancing variants: AVL tree, Red-Black tree.

cards.back: S: Single Responsibility (one reason to change). O: Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification). L: Liskov Substitution (subtypes substitutable for base types). I: Interface Segregation (small, specific interfaces). D: Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concretions).
cards.notes: Foundation of object-oriented design. Helps create maintainable, flexible, testable code.

cards.front: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
cards.back: Concurrency: Managing multiple tasks at the same time (may interleave on a single core). Parallelism: Executing multiple tasks simultaneously (requires multiple cores). Concurrency is about structure; parallelism is about execution.
cards.notes: Example: A web server handling 1000 connections concurrently on 4 cores. Each core runs requests in parallel.

cards.front: What sorting algorithm is generally fastest for average cases?
cards.back: Quicksort — Average O(n log n), worst case O(n²). In practice, fastest due to good cache performance and small constant factor. Mergesort — Guaranteed O(n log n), stable, but requires O(n) extra space.
cards.notes: Timsort (Python, Java): hybrid of mergesort + insertion sort, O(n log n). Heapsort: O(n log n) guaranteed, in-place, but poor cache performance.

cards.back: REpresentational State Transfer. Architectural style for web services using HTTP methods on resources (URLs). Stateless: each request contains all needed information. Uses standard HTTP status codes (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error).
cards.notes: Principles: client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system. Resources identified by URIs. JSON is the common format.

cards.front: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
cards.back: TCP: Connection-oriented, reliable delivery, ordered, flow control, error checking. Slower. Used for: HTTP, email, file transfer. UDP: Connectionless, unreliable (no guarantees), unordered, faster. Used for: DNS, streaming, gaming, VoIP.
cards.notes: TCP uses 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK). UDP has no handshake. TCP header: 20+ bytes. UDP header: 8 bytes.

cards.front: What is a database index and why use it?
cards.back: A data structure (typically B-tree or hash) that speeds up data retrieval on specific columns. Trades write speed and storage for faster reads. Without index: O(n) table scan. With index: O(log n) lookup.
cards.notes: Over-indexing slows writes and wastes space. Composite index: covers multiple columns. Covering index: includes all columns needed for a query.

cards.front: What is the difference between process and thread?
cards.back: Process: Independent program with its own memory space. Heavyweight, isolated. Communication via IPC. Thread: Lightweight unit of execution within a process. Shares memory space with other threads. Faster context switching.
cards.notes: Multithreading risks: race conditions, deadlocks, data corruption. Solutions: mutexes, semaphores, locks, atomic operations.

cards.front: What is Git and what are its basic commands?
cards.back: Distributed version control system. Key commands: git init (create repo), git clone (copy repo), git add (stage changes), git commit (save snapshot), git push (upload), git pull (download + merge), git branch (manage branches), git merge (combine branches).
cards.notes: Branching model: main/master for production, feature branches for development. GitHub/GitLab: hosting platforms with collaboration tools (PRs, issues).
